perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall keep confidential the Information for [five (5)] years from the date of disclosure, and shall not disclose the Information to any other person or company without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent, and shall not itself make any use of such Information for any purpose other than the purpose of supply of logistics services ("the Authorised Purpose").
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall keep confidential the Information for [five (5)] years from the date of disclosure, and may disclose the Information to any other person or company without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent, and shall not itself make any use of such Information for any purpose other than the purpose of supply of logistics services ("the Authorised Purpose").
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not disclose' to 'may disclose' creates a direct contradiction regarding the confidentiality obligation. It introduces ambiguity whether disclosure is allowed, violating the purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith (UK)
perturbation.law_explanation: In the UK, the implied duty of good faith dictates that parties should act honestly and fairly in their contractual relations. By changing 'shall not disclose' to 'may disclose', the agreement fails to clearly prevent the receiving party from disclosing proprietary information, which violates the spirit of an NDA and the duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated that the Receiving Party 'shall not disclose' information, while the changed text uses 'may disclose'. This alteration directly contradicts the original intent of confidentiality, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this violates the purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement. Even without direct law snippet support, the alteration from a mandatory prohibition to a permissive allowance fundamentally undermines the agreement's confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall: (a) take the same care in protecting the Disclosing Party's Information as it takes in protecting its own confidential information and in any event not less than that which a reasonable person or business would take in protecting its own confidential information; (b) only disclose Information on a need-to-know basis to such of their employees, agents, consultants and contractors as are under similar obligations of confidentiality as contained in this Agreement including, but not limited to, the use of the Information for the Authorised Purpose only; and (c) forthwith upon receipt of a written request from the Disclosing Party or upon termination: (i) return all information supplied by the Disclosing Party as well as items and materials relating to or derived from the Information; (ii) deliver to the Disclosing Party or at its request destroy immediately all items and materials made by the Receiving Party containing Information, that are not returned pursuant to paragraph (i) above; (iii) not keep copies or duplicates of any items or materials referred to in paragraphs (i) or (ii) above; and (iv) provide a certificate signed by a senior officer of the Receiving Party confirming that the provisions of this clause have been complied with.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall: (a) take reasonable steps in protecting the Disclosing Party's Information; (b) disclose Information as needed to their employees, agents, consultants and contractors regardless of confidentiality obligations; and (c) upon termination: (i) return all information supplied by the Disclosing Party as well as items and materials relating to or derived from the Information only if requested by the Disclosing Party; (ii) deliver to the Disclosing Party or at its request destroy items and materials made by the Receiving Party containing Information, that are not returned pursuant to paragraph (i) above; (iii) may keep copies or duplicates of any items or materials referred to in paragraphs (i) or (ii) above; and (iv) provide a certificate signed by an officer of the Receiving Party confirming compliance only if such a certificate is available.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the confidentiality requirements. Changing (a) removes the 'reasonable person' standard. Modifying (b) eliminates the need-to-know and confidentiality obligations, allowing for unrestricted disclosure. The changes to (c) regarding return, copies, and certificates make compliance optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: English law recognizes a tort of breach of confidence. By removing the requirement for similar confidentiality obligations for employees and allowing the receiving party to disclose information as needed, the change undermines the duty of confidence. The modifications regarding the return of materials, allowing copies to be kept, etc. would also make it harder to prove that a breach of confidentiality occurred.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text significantly weakens confidentiality requirements, creating a contradiction. The original text mandates protecting the Disclosing Party's Information with at least the same care a reasonable person would use for their own confidential information. It also restricts disclosure to employees under similar confidentiality obligations. The changed text removes the 'reasonable person' standard and allows disclosure 'as needed' without confidentiality constraints. While the provided law snippets are missing and cannot be used to further support this claim, the significant removal of confidentiality obligations, as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, strongly suggests a failure to maintain necessary confidentiality standards, making it likely to be unenforceable as it does not align with common law principals regarding breach of confidence.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will not be assignable or transferable by either party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be assignable or transferable by either party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not be' to 'will be' directly contradicts the original restriction on assignment and transfer. This affects the rights and obligations of the parties, potentially leading to unintended or unauthorized transfer of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While UK law generally permits assignment of contractual rights, restrictions on assignment are enforceable if clearly stated in the contract. By changing the contract to allow assignment, it removes the protection intended by the original clause and potentially introduces new parties without the Disclosing Party's consent, impacting the integrity of the NDA.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibits assignment or transfer of the agreement by either party. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating the agreement 'will be assignable or transferable by either party.' The Law Explanation supports that restrictions on assignment are enforceable if clearly stated. The modified text removes this restriction, creating a direct conflict with the original intent and potentially undermining legal protections by allowing unintended transfer of obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England and the parties submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of California and the parties submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from 'England' to 'California' while maintaining jurisdiction in 'English Courts' creates a contradiction. California law and English law differ significantly in contract interpretation and enforcement. Maintaining jurisdiction in English Courts while applying California law creates conflict of laws issues, making enforcement unpredictable. The jurisdiction is not exclusive, so the parties may be able to pursue legal action in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: Conflict of laws principles dictates that courts must determine which law applies when a contract involves multiple jurisdictions. This modification attempts to apply California law while maintaining jurisdiction in English courts. This contradiction creates uncertainty because English courts must then decide whether to apply California law as written or to interpret it through the lens of English legal principles. The choice of law and choice of forum should be aligned for clarity and predictability. The change of choice of law will create confusion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: OIG Enforcement Summary: July 1, 2022 – July 15, 2022 - Healthcare - United States ARTICLE 4 August 2022 OIG Enforcement Summary: July 1, 2022 – July 15, 2022 HB Harris Beach More Contributor Harris Beach Explore Firm Details The following is a summary of the federal Department of Health and Human Services' Office of Inspector General (OIG) reports of fraud and abuse enforcement activity across the country. United States Food, Drugs, Healthcare, Life Sciences Matthew Babcock and Heidi Schult Gregory To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The following is a summary of the federal Department of Health and Human Services' Office of Inspector General (OIG) reports of fraud and abuse enforcement activity across the country. 1 The enforcement actions reported are based upon federal and individual states' activity. The summaries reflect areas of OIG's and individual states' current and recent enforcement activity. 2 Knowing where regulators' attention is focused can help healthcare providers identify areas of focus for compliance and risk assessment activities. Although not all the enforcement actions may be relevant to any one provider's healthcare business, there may be some summaries that could be used as examples in compliance program education programs (“What to avoid”), or used in developing a risk management plan. Of Note in this Issue: Diagnostic reference lab settlement for paying above fair market value for office space to physician-lessors seen as payment for referrals. Federal and Massachusetts settlement reported. * Corporate Integrity Agreement (not yet reported) to be executed following settlements. Three year Integrity Agreement to be executed in settlement. National dialysis provider alleged to routinely perform certain procedures with End Stage Renal Disease (“ESRD”) who were receiving dialysis, without sufficient clinical indication that the patients needed the procedures. Medicare Advantage Plan settled allegations of FCA and AKS violations in its used of gift cards to induce referrals. Key: AG = Attorney General AKS = Anti-Kickback Statute CIA = Corporate Integrity Agreement CMP = Civil Monetary Penalties CMS = Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services CPT = Current Procedural Terminology Codes DME = Durable Medical Equipment E&M = Evaluation & Management services FEHBP = Federal Employees Health Benefits Program FMV = Fair Market Value DOJ = United States Department of Justice FCA = False Claims Act FWA = Fraud, Waste & Abuse HHS = Department of Health and Human Services IA = Integrity Agreement LTC = Long Term Care (usually facilities) MCO = Managed Care Organization (typically Medicaid) MFCU = Medicaid Fraud Control Unit MSO = Management Services Organization OIG = Office of Inspector General in HHS OT = Occupational Therapy PBM = Pharmacy Benefit Managers PT = Physical Therapy SNF = Skilled Nursing Facility July 15, 2022 OIG News Release Federal Health Care Fraud Charge Filed against Suburban Chicago Physician IL. Defendant, physician, allegedly prescribed opioids to patients without a medical examination or visit and then fraudulently billed Medicare for the nonexistent treatment. The Defendant is accused of pre-signing blank prescriptions for opioids for patients of her pain clinic so prescriptions could be provided to patients when she was not at the clinic and patients picked up the prescriptions without having a contemporaneous examination by the Defendant. The Defendant allegedly submitted false claims seeking reimbursement from Medicare for visits that the Defendant knew did not occur, false paperwork was created by the clinic and Defendant indicating that a face-to-face exam took place when the patients only picked up the pre-signed prescriptions and did not have an actual exam. July 14, 2022 OIG News Release Licensed Attorney Sentenced to Prison For Defrauding Medicaid in Scheme Involving Personal Care Services – Defendant Admitted Submitting False Timesheets
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by changing the governing law from 'England' to 'California' while keeping the jurisdiction in 'English Courts.' This is contradictory because California and English laws differ, especially in contract interpretation and enforcement. Maintaining English Court jurisdiction while applying California law generates conflict of laws issues, rendering enforcement unpredictable. Although the jurisdiction isn't exclusive, potentially allowing legal action in California, the primary issue is the conflict. The snippets do not directly address this situation.
